53: // We try to spread the load on apiserver by setting timeouts for
54: // watch requests - it is random in [minWatchTimeout, 2*minWatchTimeout].
55: var defaultMinWatchTimeout = 5 * time.Minute
56: 
57: // ReflectorStore is the subset of cache.Store that the reflector uses
58: type ReflectorStore interface {
59: 	// Add adds the given object to the accumulator associated with the given object's key
60: 	Add(obj interface{}) error
61: 
62: 	// Update updates the given object in the accumulator associated with the given object's key
63: 	Update(obj interface{}) error
64: 
65: 	// Delete deletes the given object from the accumulator associated with the given object's key
66: 	Delete(obj interface{}) error
67: 
68: 	// Replace will delete the contents of the store, using instead the
69: 	// given list. Store takes ownership of the list, you should not reference
70: 	// it after calling this function.
71: 	Replace([]interface{}, string) error
72: 
73: 	// Resync is meaningless in the terms appearing here but has
74: 	// meaning in some implementations that have non-trivial
75: 	// additional behavior (e.g., DeltaFIFO).
76: 	Resync() error
77: }
78: 
79: // TransformingStore is an optional interface that can be implemented by the provided store.
80: // If implemented on the provided store reflector will use the same transformer in its internal stores.
81: type TransformingStore interface {
82: 	ReflectorStore
83: 	Transformer() TransformFunc
84: }
85: 
86: // Reflector watches a specified resource and causes all changes to be reflected in the given store.
87: type Reflector struct {
88: 	// name identifies this reflector. By default, it will be a file:line if possible.
89: 	name string
90: 	// The name of the type we expect to place in the store. The name
91: 	// will be the stringification of expectedGVK if provided, and the
92: 	// stringification of expectedType otherwise. It is for display
93: 	// only, and should not be used for parsing or comparison.
94: 	typeDescription string
95: 	// An example object of the type we expect to place in the store.
96: 	// Only the type needs to be right, except that when that is
97: 	// `unstructured.Unstructured` the object's `"apiVersion"` and
98: 	// `"kind"` must also be right.
99: 	expectedType reflect.Type
100: 	// The GVK of the object we expect to place in the store if unstructured.
101: 	expectedGVK *schema.GroupVersionKind
102: 	// The destination to sync up with the watch source
103: 	store ReflectorStore
104: 	// listerWatcher is used to perform lists and watches.
105: 	listerWatcher ListerWatcherWithContext
106: 	// backoff manages backoff of ListWatch
107: 	backoffManager wait.BackoffManager
108: 	resyncPeriod   time.Duration
109: 	// minWatchTimeout defines the minimum timeout for watch requests.
110: 	minWatchTimeout time.Duration
111: 	// clock allows tests to manipulate time
112: 	clock clock.Clock
113: 	// paginatedResult defines whether pagination should be forced for list calls.
114: 	// It is set based on the result of the initial list call.
115: 	paginatedResult bool
116: 	// lastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version token last
117: 	// observed when doing a sync with the underlying store
118: 	// it is thread safe, but not synchronized with the underlying store
119: 	lastSyncResourceVersion string
120: 	// isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable is true if the previous list or watch request with
121: 	// lastSyncResourceVersion failed with an "expired" or "too large resource version" error.
122: 	isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable bool
123: 	// lastSyncResourceVersionMutex guards read/write access to lastSyncResourceVersion
124: 	lastSyncResourceVersionMutex sync.RWMutex
125: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
126: 	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
127: 	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and resync watch lists.
128: 	// If unset, for consistent reads (RV="") or reads that opt-into arbitrarily old data
129: 	// (RV="0") it will default to pager.PageSize, for the rest (RV != "" && RV != "0")
130: 	// it will turn off pagination to allow serving them from watch cache.
131: 	// NOTE: It should be used carefully as paginated lists are always served directly from
132: 	// etcd, which is significantly less efficient and may lead to serious performance and
133: 	// scalability problems.
134: 	WatchListPageSize int64
135: 	// ShouldResync is invoked periodically and whenever it returns `true` the Store's Resync operation is invoked
136: 	ShouldResync func() bool
137: 	// MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration defines how long we should retry internal errors returned by watch.
138: 	MaxInternalErrorRetryDuration time.Duration
139: 	// useWatchList if turned on instructs the reflector to open a stream to bring data from the API server.
140: 	// Streaming has the primary advantage of using fewer server's resources to fetch data.
141: 	//
142: 	// The old behaviour establishes a LIST request which gets data in chunks.
143: 	// Paginated list is less efficient and depending on the actual size of objects
144: 	// might result in an increased memory consumption of the APIServer.
145: 	//
146: 	// See https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list#design-details
147: 	useWatchList bool
148: }

158: // ResourceVersionUpdater is an interface that allows store implementation to
159: // track the current resource version of the reflector. This is especially
160: // important if storage bookmarks are enabled.
161: type ResourceVersionUpdater interface {
162: 	// UpdateResourceVersion is called each time current resource version of the reflector
163: 	// is updated.
164: 	UpdateResourceVersion(resourceVersion string)
165: }
166: 
167: // The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
168: // connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
169: // will backoff and retry.
170: //
171: // The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
172: // the error message at an appropriate level.
173: //
174: // Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
175: // ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
176: // should be offloaded.
177: type WatchErrorHandler func(r *Reflector, err error)
178: 
179: // The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the
180: // connection with an error. After calling this handler, the informer
181: // will backoff and retry.
182: //
183: // The default implementation looks at the error type and tries to log
184: // the error message at an appropriate level.
185: //
186: // Implementations of this handler may display the error message in other
187: // ways. Implementations should return quickly - any expensive processing
188: // should be offloaded.
189: type WatchErrorHandlerWithContext func(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error)
190: 
191: // DefaultWatchErrorHandler is the default implementation of WatchErrorHandlerWithContext.
192: func DefaultWatchErrorHandler(ctx context.Context, r *Reflector, err error) {
193: 	switch {
194: 	case isExpiredError(err):
195: 		// Don't set LastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable - LIST call with ResourceVersion=RV already
196: 		// has a semantic that it returns data at least as fresh as provided RV.
197: 		// So first try to LIST with setting RV to resource version of last observed object.
198: 		klog.FromContext(ctx).V(4).Info("Watch closed", "reflector", r.name, "type", r.typeDescription, "err", err)
199: 	case err == io.EOF:
200: 		// watch closed normally
201: 	case err == io.ErrUnexpectedEOF:
202: 		klog.FromContext(ctx).V(1).Info("Watch closed with unexpected EOF", "reflector", r.name, "type", r.typeDescription, "err", err)
203: 	default:
204: 		utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext(ctx, err, "Failed to watch", "reflector", r.name, "type", r.typeDescription)
205: 	}
206: }

208: // NewNamespaceKeyedIndexerAndReflector creates an Indexer and a Reflector
209: // The indexer is configured to key on namespace
210: func NewNamespaceKeyedIndexerAndReflector(lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, resyncPeriod time.Duration) (indexer Indexer, reflector *Reflector) {
211: 	indexer = NewIndexer(MetaNamespaceKeyFunc, Indexers{NamespaceIndex: MetaNamespaceIndexFunc})
212: 	reflector = NewReflector(lw, expectedType, indexer, resyncPeriod)
213: 	return indexer, reflector
214: }

216: // NewReflector creates a new Reflector with its name defaulted to the closest source_file.go:line in the call stack
217: // that is outside this package. See NewReflectorWithOptions for further information.
218: func NewReflector(lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, resyncPeriod time.Duration) *Reflector {
219: 	return NewReflectorWithOptions(lw, expectedType, store, ReflectorOptions{ResyncPeriod: resyncPeriod})
220: }
221: 
222: // NewNamedReflector creates a new Reflector with the specified name. See NewReflectorWithOptions for further
223: // information.
224: func NewNamedReflector(name string, lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, resyncPeriod time.Duration) *Reflector {
225: 	return NewReflectorWithOptions(lw, expectedType, store, ReflectorOptions{Name: name, ResyncPeriod: resyncPeriod})
226: }
227: 
228: // ReflectorOptions configures a Reflector.
229: type ReflectorOptions struct {
230: 	// Name is the Reflector's name. If unset/unspecified, the name defaults to the closest source_file.go:line
231: 	// in the call stack that is outside this package.
232: 	Name string
233: 
234: 	// TypeDescription is the Reflector's type description. If unset/unspecified, the type description is defaulted
235: 	// using the following rules: if the expectedType passed to NewReflectorWithOptions was nil, the type description is
236: 	// "<unspecified>". If the expectedType is an instance of *unstructured.Unstructured and its apiVersion and kind fields
237: 	// are set, the type description is the string encoding of those. Otherwise, the type description is set to the
238: 	// go type of expectedType..
239: 	TypeDescription string
240: 
241: 	// ResyncPeriod is the Reflector's resync period. If unset/unspecified, the resync period defaults to 0
242: 	// (do not resync).
243: 	ResyncPeriod time.Duration
244: 
245: 	// MinWatchTimeout, if non-zero, defines the minimum timeout for watch requests send to kube-apiserver.
246: 	// However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid negative performance impact on controlplane.
247: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
248: 
249: 	// Clock allows tests to control time. If unset defaults to clock.RealClock{}
250: 	Clock clock.Clock
251: }
252: 
253: // NewReflectorWithOptions creates a new Reflector object which will keep the
254: // given store up to date with the server's contents for the given
255: // resource. Reflector promises to only put things in the store that
256: // have the type of expectedType, unless expectedType is nil. If
257: // resyncPeriod is non-zero, then the reflector will periodically
258: // consult its ShouldResync function to determine whether to invoke
259: // the Store's Resync operation; `ShouldResync==nil` means always
260: // "yes".  This enables you to use reflectors to periodically process
261: // everything as well as incrementally processing the things that
262: // change.
263: func NewReflectorWithOptions(lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, options ReflectorOptions) *Reflector {
264: 	reflectorClock := options.Clock
265: 	if reflectorClock == nil {
266: 		reflectorClock = clock.RealClock{}
267: 	}
268: 	minWatchTimeout := defaultMinWatchTimeout
269: 	if options.MinWatchTimeout > defaultMinWatchTimeout {
270: 		minWatchTimeout = options.MinWatchTimeout
271: 	}
272: 	r := &Reflector{
273: 		name:            options.Name,
274: 		resyncPeriod:    options.ResyncPeriod,
275: 		minWatchTimeout: minWatchTimeout,
276: 		typeDescription: options.TypeDescription,
277: 		listerWatcher:   ToListerWatcherWithContext(lw),
278: 		store:           store,
279: 		// We used to make the call every 1sec (1 QPS), the goal here is to achieve ~98% traffic reduction when
280: 		// API server is not healthy. With these parameters, backoff will stop at [30,60) sec interval which is
281: 		// 0.22 QPS. If we don't backoff for 2min, assume API server is healthy and we reset the backoff.
282: 		backoffManager:    wait.NewExponentialBackoffManager(800*time.Millisecond, 30*time.Second, 2*time.Minute, 2.0, 1.0, reflectorClock),
283: 		clock:             reflectorClock,
284: 		watchErrorHandler: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext(DefaultWatchErrorHandler),
285: 		expectedType:      reflect.TypeOf(expectedType),
286: 	}
287: 
288: 	if r.name == "" {
289: 		r.name = naming.GetNameFromCallsite(internalPackages...)
290: 	}
291: 
292: 	if r.typeDescription == "" {
293: 		r.typeDescription = getTypeDescriptionFromObject(expectedType)
294: 	}
295: 
296: 	if r.expectedGVK == nil {
297: 		r.expectedGVK = getExpectedGVKFromObject(expectedType)
298: 	}
299: 
300: 	r.useWatchList = clientfeatures.FeatureGates().Enabled(clientfeatures.WatchListClient)
301: 	if r.useWatchList && watchlist.DoesClientNotSupportWatchListSemantics(lw) {
302: 		// Using klog.TODO() here because switching to a caller-provided contextual logger
303: 		// would require an API change and updating all existing call sites.
304: 		klog.TODO().V(2).Info(
305: 			"The client used to build this informer/reflector doesn't support WatchList semantics. The feature will be disabled. This is expected in unit tests but not in production. For details, see the documentation of watchlist.DoesClientNotSupportWatchListSemantics().",
306: 			"feature", clientfeatures.WatchListClient,
307: 		)
308: 		r.useWatchList = false
309: 	}
310: 
311: 	return r
312: }
313: 
314: func getTypeDescriptionFromObject(expectedType interface{}) string {
315: 	if expectedType == nil {
316: 		return defaultExpectedTypeName
317: 	}
318: 
319: 	reflectDescription := reflect.TypeOf(expectedType).String()
320: 
321: 	obj, ok := expectedType.(*unstructured.Unstructured)
322: 	if !ok {
323: 		return reflectDescription
324: 	}
325: 
326: 	gvk := obj.GroupVersionKind()
327: 	if gvk.Empty() {
328: 		return reflectDescription
329: 	}
330: 
331: 	return gvk.String()
332: }
333: 
334: func getExpectedGVKFromObject(expectedType interface{}) *schema.GroupVersionKind {
335: 	obj, ok := expectedType.(*unstructured.Unstructured)
336: 	if !ok {
337: 		return nil
338: 	}
339: 
340: 	gvk := obj.GroupVersionKind()
341: 	if gvk.Empty() {
342: 		return nil
343: 	}
344: 
345: 	return &gvk
346: }
347: 
348: // internalPackages are packages that ignored when creating a default reflector name. These packages are in the common
349: // call chains to NewReflector, so they'd be low entropy names for reflectors
350: var internalPackages = []string{"client-go/tools/cache/"}
351: 
352: // Run repeatedly uses the reflector's ListAndWatch to fetch all the
353: // objects and subsequent deltas.
354: // Run will exit when stopCh is closed.
355: //
356: // Contextual logging: RunWithContext should be used instead of Run in code which supports contextual logging.
357: func (r *Reflector) Run(stopCh <-chan struct{}) {
358: 	r.RunWithContext(wait.ContextForChannel(stopCh))
359: }
360: 
361: // RunWithContext repeatedly uses the reflector's ListAndWatch to fetch all the
362: // objects and subsequent deltas.
363: // Run will exit when the context is canceled.
364: func (r *Reflector) RunWithContext(ctx context.Context) {
365: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
366: 	logger.V(3).Info("Starting reflector", "type", r.typeDescription, "resyncPeriod", r.resyncPeriod, "reflector", r.name)
367: 	wait.BackoffUntil(func() {
368: 		if err := r.ListAndWatchWithContext(ctx); err != nil {
369: 			r.watchErrorHandler(ctx, r, err)
370: 		}
371: 	}, r.backoffManager, true, ctx.Done())
372: 	logger.V(3).Info("Stopping reflector", "type", r.typeDescription, "resyncPeriod", r.resyncPeriod, "reflector", r.name)
373: }
374: 
375: var (
376: 	// Used to indicate that watching stopped because of a signal from the stop
377: 	// channel passed in from a client of the reflector.
378: 	errorStopRequested = errors.New("stop requested")
379: )
380: 
381: // resyncChan returns a channel which will receive something when a resync is
382: // required, and a cleanup function.
383: func (r *Reflector) resyncChan() (<-chan time.Time, func() bool) {
384: 	if r.resyncPeriod == 0 {
385: 		// nothing will ever be sent down this channel
386: 		return nil, func() bool { return false }
387: 	}
388: 	// The cleanup function is required: imagine the scenario where watches
389: 	// always fail so we end up listing frequently. Then, if we don't
390: 	// manually stop the timer, we could end up with many timers active
391: 	// concurrently.
392: 	t := r.clock.NewTimer(r.resyncPeriod)
393: 	return t.C(), t.Stop
394: }
395: 
396: // ListAndWatch first lists all items and get the resource version at the moment of call,
397: // and then use the resource version to watch.
398: // It returns error if ListAndWatch didn't even try to initialize watch.
399: //
400: // Contextual logging: ListAndWatchWithContext should be used instead of ListAndWatch in code which supports contextual logging.
401: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kubernetes--stripped@6d5678ae074d17945f174da2b064603131370a5f/-/blob/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go%3FL150-400"}

28: 	utilruntime "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"

40: // SharedInformer provides eventually consistent linkage of its
41: // clients to the authoritative state of a given collection of
42: // objects.  An object is identified by its API group, kind/resource,
43: // namespace (if any), and name; the `ObjectMeta.UID` is not part of
44: // an object's ID as far as this contract is concerned.  One
45: // SharedInformer provides linkage to objects of a particular API
46: // group and kind/resource.  The linked object collection of a
47: // SharedInformer may be further restricted to one namespace (if
48: // applicable) and/or by label selector and/or field selector.
49: //
50: // The authoritative state of an object is what apiservers provide
51: // access to, and an object goes through a strict sequence of states.
52: // An object state is either (1) present with a ResourceVersion and
53: // other appropriate content or (2) "absent".
54: //
55: // A SharedInformer maintains a local cache --- exposed by GetStore(),
56: // by GetIndexer() in the case of an indexed informer, and possibly by
57: // machinery involved in creating and/or accessing the informer --- of
58: // the state of each relevant object.  This cache is eventually
59: // consistent with the authoritative state.  This means that, unless
60: // prevented by persistent communication problems, if ever a
61: // particular object ID X is authoritatively associated with a state S
62: // then for every SharedInformer I whose collection includes (X, S)
63: // eventually either (1) I's cache associates X with S or a later
64: // state of X, (2) I is stopped, or (3) the authoritative state
65: // service for X terminates.  To be formally complete, we say that the
66: // absent state meets any restriction by label selector or field
67: // selector.
68: //
69: // For a given informer and relevant object ID X, the sequence of
70: // states that appears in the informer's cache is a subsequence of the
71: // states authoritatively associated with X.  That is, some states
72: // might never appear in the cache but ordering among the appearing
73: // states is correct.  Note, however, that there is no promise about
74: // ordering between states seen for different objects.
75: //
76: // The local cache starts out empty, and gets populated and updated
77: // during `Run()`.
78: //
79: // As a simple example, if a collection of objects is henceforth
80: // unchanging, a SharedInformer is created that links to that
81: // collection, and that SharedInformer is `Run()` then that
82: // SharedInformer's cache eventually holds an exact copy of that
83: // collection (unless it is stopped too soon, the authoritative state
84: // service ends, or communication problems between the two
85: // persistently thwart achievement).
86: //
87: // As another simple example, if the local cache ever holds a
88: // non-absent state for some object ID and the object is eventually
89: // removed from the authoritative state then eventually the object is
90: // removed from the local cache (unless the SharedInformer is stopped
91: // too soon, the authoritative state service ends, or communication
92: // problems persistently thwart the desired result).
93: //
94: // The keys in the Store are of the form namespace/name for namespaced
95: // objects, and are simply the name for non-namespaced objects.
96: // Clients can use `MetaNamespaceKeyFunc(obj)` to extract the key for
97: // a given object, and `SplitMetaNamespaceKey(key)` to split a key
98: // into its constituent parts.
99: //
100: // Every query against the local cache is answered entirely from one
101: // snapshot of the cache's state.  Thus, the result of a `List` call
102: // will not contain two entries with the same namespace and name.
103: //
104: // A client is identified here by a ResourceEventHandler.  For every
105: // update to the SharedInformer's local cache and for every client
106: // added before `Run()`, eventually either the SharedInformer is
107: // stopped or the client is notified of the update.  A client added
108: // after `Run()` starts gets a startup batch of notifications of
109: // additions of the objects existing in the cache at the time that
110: // client was added; also, for every update to the SharedInformer's
111: // local cache after that client was added, eventually either the
112: // SharedInformer is stopped or that client is notified of that
113: // update.  Client notifications happen after the corresponding cache
114: // update and, in the case of a SharedIndexInformer, after the
115: // corresponding index updates.  It is possible that additional cache
116: // and index updates happen before such a prescribed notification.
117: // For a given SharedInformer and client, the notifications are
118: // delivered sequentially.  For a given SharedInformer, client, and
119: // object ID, the notifications are delivered in order.  Because
120: // `ObjectMeta.UID` has no role in identifying objects, it is possible
121: // that when (1) object O1 with ID (e.g. namespace and name) X and
122: // `ObjectMeta.UID` U1 in the SharedInformer's local cache is deleted
123: // and later (2) another object O2 with ID X and ObjectMeta.UID U2 is
124: // created the informer's clients are not notified of (1) and (2) but
125: // rather are notified only of an update from O1 to O2. Clients that
126: // need to detect such cases might do so by comparing the `ObjectMeta.UID`
127: // field of the old and the new object in the code that handles update
128: // notifications (i.e. `OnUpdate` method of ResourceEventHandler).
129: //
130: // A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer
131: // is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of
132: // notifications to deliver.  Lengthy processing should be passed off
133: // to something else, for example through a
134: // `client-go/util/workqueue`.
135: //
136: // A delete notification exposes the last locally known non-absent
137: // state, except that its ResourceVersion is replaced with a
138: // ResourceVersion in which the object is actually absent.
139: type SharedInformer interface {
140: 	// AddEventHandler adds an event handler to the shared informer using
141: 	// the shared informer's resync period.  Events to a single handler are
142: 	// delivered sequentially, but there is no coordination between
143: 	// different handlers.
144: 	// It returns a registration handle for the handler that can be used to
145: 	// remove the handler again, or to tell if the handler is synced (has
146: 	// seen every item in the initial list).
147: 	//
148: 	// Contextual logging: AddEventHandlerWithOptions together with a logger in the options should be used instead of AddEventHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
149: 	AddEventHandler(handler ResourceEventHandler) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
150: 	// AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod adds an event handler to the
151: 	// shared informer with the requested resync period; zero means
152: 	// this handler does not care about resyncs.  The resync operation
153: 	// consists of delivering to the handler an update notification
154: 	// for every object in the informer's local cache; it does not add
155: 	// any interactions with the authoritative storage.  Some
156: 	// informers do no resyncs at all, not even for handlers added
157: 	// with a non-zero resyncPeriod.  For an informer that does
158: 	// resyncs, and for each handler that requests resyncs, that
159: 	// informer develops a nominal resync period that is no shorter
160: 	// than the requested period but may be longer.  The actual time
161: 	// between any two resyncs may be longer than the nominal period
162: 	// because the implementation takes time to do work and there may
163: 	// be competing load and scheduling noise.
164: 	// It returns a registration handle for the handler that can be used to remove
165: 	// the handler again and an error if the handler cannot be added.
166: 	//
167: 	// Contextual logging: AddEventHandlerWithOptions together with a logger in the options should be used instead of AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod in code which supports contextual logging.
168: 	AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod(handler ResourceEventHandler, resyncPeriod time.Duration) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
169: 	// AddEventHandlerWithOptions is a variant of AddEventHandlerWithResyncPeriod where
170: 	// all optional parameters are passed in a struct.
171: 	AddEventHandlerWithOptions(handler ResourceEventHandler, options HandlerOptions) (ResourceEventHandlerRegistration, error)
172: 	// RemoveEventHandler removes a formerly added event handler given by
173: 	// its registration handle.
174: 	// This function is guaranteed to be idempotent, and thread-safe.
175: 	RemoveEventHandler(handle ResourceEventHandlerRegistration) error
176: 	// GetStore returns the informer's local cache as a Store.
177: 	GetStore() Store
178: 	// GetController is deprecated, it does nothing useful
179: 	GetController() Controller
180: 	// Run starts and runs the shared informer, returning after it stops.
181: 	// The informer will be stopped when stopCh is closed.
182: 	//
183: 	// Contextual logging: RunWithContext should be used instead of Run in code which uses contextual logging.
184: 	Run(stopCh <-chan struct{})
185: 	// RunWithContext starts and runs the shared informer, returning after it stops.
186: 	// The informer will be stopped when the context is canceled.
187: 	RunWithContext(ctx context.Context)
188: 	// HasSynced returns true if the shared informer's store has been
189: 	// informed by at least one full LIST of the authoritative state
190: 	// of the informer's object collection.  This is unrelated to "resync".
191: 	//
192: 	// Note that this doesn't tell you if an individual handler is synced!!
193: 	// For that, please call HasSynced on the handle returned by
194: 	// AddEventHandler.
195: 	HasSynced() bool
196: 	// LastSyncResourceVersion is the resource version observed when last synced with the underlying
197: 	// store. The value returned is not synchronized with access to the underlying store and is not
198: 	// thread-safe.
199: 	LastSyncResourceVersion() string
200:

31: // DeltaFIFOOptions is the configuration parameters for DeltaFIFO. All are
32: // optional.
33: type DeltaFIFOOptions struct {
34: 
35: 	// KeyFunction is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It's
36: 	// exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional
37: 	// handling around deleted objects and queue state).
38: 	// Optional, the default is MetaNamespaceKeyFunc.
39: 	KeyFunction KeyFunc
40: 
41: 	// KnownObjects is expected to return a list of keys that the consumer of
42: 	// this queue "knows about". It is used to decide which items are missing
43: 	// when Replace() is called; 'Deleted' deltas are produced for the missing items.
44: 	// KnownObjects may be nil if you can tolerate missing deletions on Replace().
45: 	KnownObjects KeyListerGetter
46: 
47: 	// EmitDeltaTypeReplaced indicates that the queue consumer
48: 	// understands the Replaced DeltaType. Before the `Replaced` event type was
49: 	// added, calls to Replace() were handled the same as Sync(). For
50: 	// backwards-compatibility purposes, this is false by default.
51: 	// When true, `Replaced` events will be sent for items passed to a Replace() call.
52: 	// When false, `Sync` events will be sent instead.
53: 	EmitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
54: 
55: 	// If set, will be called for objects before enqueueing them. Please
56: 	// see the comment on TransformFunc for details.
57: 	Transformer TransformFunc
58: 
59: 	// If set, log output will go to this logger instead of klog.Background().
60: 	Logger *klog.Logger
61: }
62: 
63: // DeltaFIFO is like FIFO, but differs in two ways.  One is that the
64: // accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object
65: // but rather a Deltas, which is a slice of Delta values for that
66: // object.  Applying an object to a Deltas means to append a Delta
67: // except when the potentially appended Delta is a Deleted and the
68: // Deltas already ends with a Deleted.  In that case the Deltas does
69: // not grow, although the terminal Deleted will be replaced by the new
70: // Deleted if the older Deleted's object is a
71: // DeletedFinalStateUnknown.
72: //
73: // The other difference is that DeltaFIFO has two additional ways that
74: // an object can be applied to an accumulator: Replaced and Sync.
75: // If EmitDeltaTypeReplaced is not set to true, Sync will be used in
76: // replace events for backwards compatibility.  Sync is used for periodic
77: // resync events.
78: //
79: // DeltaFIFO is a producer-consumer queue, where a Reflector is
80: // intended to be the producer, and the consumer is whatever calls
81: // the Pop() method.
82: //
83: // DeltaFIFO solves this use case:
84: //   - You want to process every object change (delta) at most once.
85: //   - When you process an object, you want to see everything
86: //     that's happened to it since you last processed it.
87: //   - You want to process the deletion of some of the objects.
88: //   - You might want to periodically reprocess objects.
89: //
90: // DeltaFIFO's Pop(), Get(), and GetByKey() methods return
91: // interface{} to satisfy the Store/Queue interfaces, but they
92: // will always return an object of type Deltas. List() returns
93: // the newest object from each accumulator in the FIFO.
94: //
95: // A DeltaFIFO's knownObjects KeyListerGetter provides the abilities
96: // to list Store keys and to get objects by Store key.  The objects in
97: // question are called "known objects" and this set of objects
98: // modifies the behavior of the Delete, Replace, and Resync methods
99: // (each in a different way).
100: //
101: // A note on threading: If you call Pop() in parallel from multiple
102: // threads, you could end up with multiple threads processing slightly
103: // different versions of the same object.
104: type DeltaFIFO struct {
105: 	// lock/cond protects access to 'items' and 'queue'.
106: 	lock sync.RWMutex
107: 	cond sync.Cond
108: 
109: 	// `items` maps a key to a Deltas.
110: 	// Each such Deltas has at least one Delta.
111: 	items map[string]Deltas
112: 
113: 	// `queue` maintains FIFO order of keys for consumption in Pop().
114: 	// There are no duplicates in `queue`.
115: 	// A key is in `queue` if and only if it is in `items`.
116: 	queue []string
117: 
118: 	// populated is true if the first batch of items inserted by Replace() has been populated
119: 	// or Delete/Add/Update/AddIfNotPresent was called first.
120: 	populated bool
121: 	// initialPopulationCount is the number of items inserted by the first call of Replace()
122: 	initialPopulationCount int
123: 
124: 	// keyFunc is used to make the key used for queued item
125: 	// insertion and retrieval, and should be deterministic.
126: 	keyFunc KeyFunc
127: 
128: 	// knownObjects list keys that are "known" --- affecting Delete(),
129: 	// Replace(), and Resync()
130: 	knownObjects KeyListerGetter
131: 
132: 	// Used to indicate a queue is closed so a control loop can exit when a queue is empty.
133: 	// Currently, not used to gate any of CRUD operations.
134: 	closed bool
135: 
136: 	// emitDeltaTypeReplaced is whether to emit the Replaced or Sync
137: 	// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).
138: 	emitDeltaTypeReplaced bool
139: 
140: 	// Called with every object if non-nil.
141: 	transformer TransformFunc
142: 
143: 	// logger is a per-instance logger. This gets chosen when constructing
144: 	// the instance, with klog.Background() as default.
145: 	logger klog.Logger
146: }

13: See the License for the specific language governing permissions and

16: 
17: package workqueue
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"sync"
21: 	"time"
22: 
23: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"
24: 	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
25: )
26: 
27: // Deprecated: Interface is deprecated, use TypedInterface instead.
28: type Interface TypedInterface[any]
29: 
30: type TypedInterface[T comparable] interface {
31: 	Add(item T)
32: 	Len() int
33: 	Get() (item T, shutdown bool)
34: 	Done(item T)
35: 	ShutDown()
36: 	ShutDownWithDrain()
37: 	ShuttingDown() bool
38: }
39: 
40: // Queue is the underlying storage for items. The functions below are always
41: // called from the same goroutine.
42: type Queue[T comparable] interface {
43: 	// Touch can be hooked when an existing item is added again. This may be
44: 	// useful if the implementation allows priority change for the given item.
45: 	Touch(item T)
46: 	// Push adds a new item.
47: 	Push(item T)
48: 	// Len tells the total number of items.
49: 	Len() int
50: 	// Pop retrieves an item.
51: 	Pop() (item T)
52: }
53: 
54: // DefaultQueue is a slice based FIFO queue.
55: func DefaultQueue[T comparable]() Queue[T] {
56: 	return new(queue[T])
57: }
58: 
59: // queue is a slice which implements Queue.
60: type queue[T comparable] []T
61: 
62: func (q *queue[T]) Touch(item T) {}
63: 
64: func (q *queue[T]) Push(item T) {
65: 	*q = append(*q, item)
66: }
67: 
68: func (q *queue[T]) Len() int {
69: 	return len(*q)
70: }
71: 
72: func (q *queue[T]) Pop() (item T) {
73: 	item = (*q)[0]
74: 
75: 	// The underlying array still exists and reference this object, so the object will not be garbage collected.
76: 	(*q)[0] = *new(T)
77: 	*q = (*q)[1:]
78: 
79: 	return item
80: }
81: 
82: // QueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize an Interface.
83: // Deprecated: use TypedQueueConfig instead.
84: type QueueConfig = TypedQueueConfig[any]
85: 
86: type TypedQueueConfig[T comparable] struct {
87: 	// Name for the queue. If unnamed, the metrics will not be registered.
88: 	Name string
89: 
90: 	// MetricsProvider optionally allows specifying a metrics provider to use for the queue
91: 	// instead of the global provider.
92: 	MetricsProvider MetricsProvider
93: 
94: 	// Clock ability to inject real or fake clock for testing purposes.
95: 	Clock clock.WithTicker
96: 
97: 	// Queue provides the underlying queue to use. It is optional and defaults to slice based FIFO queue.
98: 	Queue Queue[T]
99: }
100: 
101: // New constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).
102: //
103: // Deprecated: use NewTyped instead.
104: func New() *Type {
105: 	return NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{
106: 		Name: "",
107: 	})
108: }
109: 
110: // NewTyped constructs a new work queue (see the package comment).
111: func NewTyped[T comparable]() *Typed[T] {
112: 	return NewTypedWithConfig(TypedQueueConfig[T]{
113: 		Name: "",
114: 	})
115: }
116: 
117: // NewWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with ability to
118: // customize different properties.
119: //
120: // Deprecated: use NewTypedWithConfig instead.
121: func NewWithConfig(config QueueConfig) *Type {
122: 	return NewTypedWithConfig(config)
123: }
124: 
125: // NewTypedWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with ability to
126: // customize different properties.
127: func NewTypedWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T]) *Typed[T] {
128: 	return newQueueWithConfig(config, defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod)
129: }
130: 
131: // NewNamed creates a new named queue.
132: // Deprecated: Use NewWithConfig instead.
133: func NewNamed(name string) *Type {
134: 	return NewWithConfig(QueueConfig{
135: 		Name: name,
136: 	})
137: }
138: 
139: // newQueueWithConfig constructs a new named workqueue
140: // with the ability to customize different properties for testing purposes
141: func newQueueWithConfig[T comparable](config TypedQueueConfig[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {
142: 	metricsProvider := globalMetricsProvider
143: 	if config.MetricsProvider != nil {
144: 		metricsProvider = config.MetricsProvider
145: 	}
146: 
147: 	if config.Clock == nil {
148: 		config.Clock = clock.RealClock{}
149: 	}
150: 
151: 	if config.Queue == nil {
152: 		config.Queue = DefaultQueue[T]()
153: 	}
154: 
155: 	return newQueue(
156: 		config.Clock,
157: 		config.Queue,
158: 		newQueueMetrics[T](metricsProvider, config.Name, config.Clock),
159: 		updatePeriod,
160: 	)
161: }
162: 
163: func newQueue[T comparable](c clock.WithTicker, queue Queue[T], metrics queueMetrics[T], updatePeriod time.Duration) *Typed[T] {
164: 	t := &Typed[T]{
165: 		clock:                      c,
166: 		queue:                      queue,
167: 		dirty:                      sets.Set[T]{},
168: 		processing:                 sets.Set[T]{},
169: 		cond:                       sync.NewCond(&sync.Mutex{}),
170: 		metrics:                    metrics,
171: 		unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod: updatePeriod,
172: 		stopCh:                     make(chan struct{}),
173: 	}
174: 
175: 	// Don't start the goroutine for a type of noMetrics so we don't consume
176: 	// resources unnecessarily
177: 	if _, ok := metrics.(noMetrics[T]); !ok {
178: 		t.wg.Go(t.updateUnfinishedWorkLoop)
179: 	}
180: 
181: 	return t
182: }
183: 
184: const defaultUnfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod = 500 * time.Millisecond
185: 
186: // Type is a work queue (see the package comment).
187: // Deprecated: Use Typed instead.
188: type Type = Typed[any]
189: 
190: type Typed[t comparable] struct {
191: 	// queue defines the order in which we will work on items. Every
192: 	// element of queue should be in the dirty set and not in the
193: 	// processing set.
194: 	queue Queue[t]
195: 
196: 	// dirty defines all of the items that need to be processed.
197: 	dirty sets.Set[t]
198: 
199: 	// Things that are currently being processed are in the processing set.
200: 	// These things may be simultaneously in the dirty set. When we finish
201: 	// processing something and remove it from this set, we'll check if
202: 	// it's in the dirty set, and if so, add it to the queue.
203: 	processing sets.Set[t]
204: 
205: 	cond *sync.Cond
206: 
207: 	shuttingDown bool
208: 	drain        bool
209: 
210: 	metrics queueMetrics[t]
211: 
212: 	unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod time.Duration
213: 	clock                      clock.WithTicker
214: 
215: 	// wg manages goroutines started by the queue to allow graceful shutdown
216: 	// ShutDown() will wait for goroutines to exit before returning.
217: 	wg sync.WaitGroup
218: 
219: 	stopCh chan struct{}
220: 	// stopOnce guarantees we only signal shutdown a single time
221: 	stopOnce sync.Once
222: }
223: 
224: // Add marks item as needing processing. When the queue is shutdown new
225: // items will silently be ignored and not queued or marked as dirty for
226: // reprocessing.
227: func (q *Typed[T]) Add(item T) {
228: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
229: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
230: 	if q.shuttingDown {
231: 		return
232: 	}
233: 	if q.dirty.Has(item) {
234: 		// the same item is added again before it is processed, call the Touch
235: 		// function if the queue cares about it (for e.g, reset its priority)
236: 		if !q.processing.Has(item) {
237: 			q.queue.Touch(item)
238: 		}
239: 		return
240: 	}
241: 
242: 	q.metrics.add(item)
243: 
244: 	q.dirty.Insert(item)
245: 	if q.processing.Has(item) {
246: 		return
247: 	}
248: 
249: 	q.queue.Push(item)
250: 	q.cond.Signal()
251: }
252: 
253: // Len returns the current queue length, for informational purposes only. You
254: // shouldn't e.g. gate a call to Add() or Get() on Len() being a particular
255: // value, that can't be synchronized properly.
256: func (q *Typed[T]) Len() int {
257: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
258: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
259: 	return q.queue.Len()
260: }
261: 
262: // Get blocks until it can return an item to be processed. If shutdown = true,
263: // the caller should end their goroutine. You must call Done with item when you
264: // have finished processing it.
265: func (q *Typed[T]) Get() (item T, shutdown bool) {
266: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
267: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
268: 	for q.queue.Len() == 0 && !q.shuttingDown {
269: 		q.cond.Wait()
270: 	}
271: 	if q.queue.Len() == 0 {
272: 		// We must be shutting down.
273: 		return *new(T), true
274: 	}
275: 
276: 	item = q.queue.Pop()
277: 
278: 	q.metrics.get(item)
279: 
280: 	q.processing.Insert(item)
281: 	q.dirty.Delete(item)
282: 
283: 	return item, false
284: }
285: 
286: // Done marks item as done processing, and if it has been marked as dirty again
287: // while it was being processed, it will be re-added to the queue for
288: // re-processing.
289: func (q *Typed[T]) Done(item T) {
290: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
291: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
292: 
293: 	q.metrics.done(item)
294: 
295: 	q.processing.Delete(item)
296: 	if q.dirty.Has(item) {
297: 		q.queue.Push(item)
298: 		q.cond.Signal()
299: 	} else if q.processing.Len() == 0 {
300: 		q.cond.Signal()
301: 	}
302: }
303: 
304: // ShutDown will cause q to ignore all new items added to it. Worker
305: // goroutines will continue processing items in the queue until it is
306: // empty and then receive the shutdown signal.
307: func (q *Typed[T]) ShutDown() {
308: 	defer q.wg.Wait()
309: 	q.stopOnce.Do(func() {
310: 		defer close(q.stopCh)
311: 	})
312: 
313: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
314: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
315: 
316: 	q.drain = false
317: 	q.shuttingDown = true
318: 	q.cond.Broadcast()
319: }
320: 
321: // ShutDownWithDrain is equivalent to ShutDown but waits until all items
322: // in the queue have been processed.
323: // ShutDown can be called after ShutDownWithDrain to force
324: // ShutDownWithDrain to stop waiting.
325: // Workers must call Done on an item after processing it, otherwise
326: // ShutDownWithDrain will block indefinitely.
327: func (q *Typed[T]) ShutDownWithDrain() {
328: 	defer q.wg.Wait()
329: 	q.stopOnce.Do(func() {
330: 		defer close(q.stopCh)
331: 	})
332: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
333: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
334: 
335: 	q.drain = true
336: 	q.shuttingDown = true
337: 	q.cond.Broadcast()
338: 
339: 	for q.processing.Len() != 0 && q.drain {
340: 		q.cond.Wait()
341: 	}
342: }
343: 
344: func (q *Typed[T]) ShuttingDown() bool {
345: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
346: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
347: 
348: 	return q.shuttingDown
349: }
350: 
351: func (q *Typed[T]) updateUnfinishedWork() {
352: 	q.cond.L.Lock()
353: 	defer q.cond.L.Unlock()
354: 	if !q.shuttingDown {
355: 		q.metrics.updateUnfinishedWork()
356: 	}
357: }
358: 
359: func (q *Typed[T]) updateUnfinishedWorkLoop() {
360: 	t := q.clock.NewTicker(q.unfinishedWorkUpdatePeriod)
361: 	defer t.Stop()
362: 	for {
363: 		select {
364: 		case <-t.C():
365: 			q.updateUnfinishedWork()
366: 		case <-q.stopCh:
367: 			return
368: 		}
369: 	}
370: }

17: package workqueue
18: 
19: import "k8s.io/utils/clock"
20: 
21: // RateLimitingInterface is an interface that rate limits items being added to the queue.
22: //
23: // Deprecated: Use TypedRateLimitingInterface instead.
24: type RateLimitingInterface TypedRateLimitingInterface[any]
25: 
26: // TypedRateLimitingInterface is an interface that rate limits items being added to the queue.
27: type TypedRateLimitingInterface[T comparable] interface {
28: 	TypedDelayingInterface[T]
29: 
30: 	// AddRateLimited adds an item to the workqueue after the rate limiter says it's ok
31: 	AddRateLimited(item T)
32: 
33: 	// Forget indicates that an item is finished being retried.  Doesn't matter whether it's for perm failing
34: 	// or for success, we'll stop the rate limiter from tracking it.  This only clears the `rateLimiter`, you
35: 	// still have to call `Done` on the queue.
36: 	Forget(item T)
37: 
38: 	// NumRequeues returns back how many times the item was requeued
39: 	NumRequeues(item T) int
40: }
41: 
42: // RateLimitingQueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize a RateLimitingInterface.
43: //
44: // Deprecated: Use TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig instead.
45: type RateLimitingQueueConfig = TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[any]
46: 
47: // TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig specifies optional configurations to customize a TypedRateLimitingInterface.
48: type TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[T comparable] struct {
49: 	// Name for the queue. If unnamed, the metrics will not be registered.
50: 	Name string
51: 
52: 	// MetricsProvider optionally allows specifying a metrics provider to use for the queue
53: 	// instead of the global provider.
54: 	MetricsProvider MetricsProvider
55: 
56: 	// Clock optionally allows injecting a real or fake clock for testing purposes.
57: 	Clock clock.WithTicker
58: 
59: 	// DelayingQueue optionally allows injecting custom delaying queue DelayingInterface instead of the default one.
60: 	DelayingQueue TypedDelayingInterface[T]
61: }
62: 
63: // NewRateLimitingQueue constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
64: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
65: // NewRateLimitingQueue does not emit metrics. For use with a MetricsProvider, please use
66: // NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead and specify a name.
67: //
68: // Deprecated: Use NewTypedRateLimitingQueue instead.
69: func NewRateLimitingQueue(rateLimiter RateLimiter) RateLimitingInterface {
70: 	return NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, RateLimitingQueueConfig{})
71: }
72: 
73: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueue constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
74: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
75: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueue does not emit metrics. For use with a MetricsProvider, please use
76: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead and specify a name.
77: func NewTypedRateLimitingQueue[T comparable](rateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[T]) TypedRateLimitingInterface[T] {
78: 	return NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[T]{})
79: }
80: 
81: // NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
82: // with options to customize different properties.
83: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
84: //
85: // Deprecated: Use NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead.
86: func NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter RateLimiter, config RateLimitingQueueConfig) RateLimitingInterface {
87: 	return NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, config)
88: }
89: 
90: // NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig constructs a new workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
91: // with options to customize different properties.
92: // Remember to call Forget!  If you don't, you may end up tracking failures forever.
93: func NewTypedRateLimitingQueueWithConfig[T comparable](rateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[T], config TypedRateLimitingQueueConfig[T]) TypedRateLimitingInterface[T] {
94: 	if config.Clock == nil {
95: 		config.Clock = clock.RealClock{}
96: 	}
97: 
98: 	if config.DelayingQueue == nil {
99: 		config.DelayingQueue = NewTypedDelayingQueueWithConfig(TypedDelayingQueueConfig[T]{
100: 			Name:            config.Name,
101: 			MetricsProvider: config.MetricsProvider,
102: 			Clock:           config.Clock,
103: 		})
104: 	}
105: 
106: 	return &rateLimitingType[T]{
107: 		TypedDelayingInterface: config.DelayingQueue,
108: 		rateLimiter:            rateLimiter,
109: 	}
110: }
111: 
112: // NewNamedRateLimitingQueue constructs a new named workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability.
113: // Deprecated: Use NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead.
114: func NewNamedRateLimitingQueue(rateLimiter RateLimiter, name string) RateLimitingInterface {
115: 	return NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, RateLimitingQueueConfig{
116: 		Name: name,
117: 	})
118: }
119: 
120: // NewRateLimitingQueueWithDelayingInterface constructs a new named workqueue with rateLimited queuing ability
121: // with the option to inject a custom delaying queue instead of the default one.
122: // Deprecated: Use NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig instead.
123: func NewRateLimitingQueueWithDelayingInterface(di DelayingInterface, rateLimiter RateLimiter) RateLimitingInterface {
124: 	return NewRateLimitingQueueWithConfig(rateLimiter, RateLimitingQueueConfig{
125: 		DelayingQueue: di,
126: 	})
127: }
128: 
129: // rateLimitingType wraps an Interface and provides rateLimited re-enquing
130: type rateLimitingType[T comparable] struct {
131: 	TypedDelayingInterface[T]
132: 
133: 	rateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[T]
134: }
135: 
136: // AddRateLimited AddAfter's the item based on the time when the rate limiter says it's ok
137: func (q *rateLimitingType[T]) AddRateLimited(item T) {
138: 	q.TypedDelayingInterface.AddAfter(item, q.rateLimiter.When(item))
139: }
140: 
141: func (q *rateLimitingType[T]) NumRequeues(item T) int {
142: 	return q.rateLimiter.NumRequeues(item)
143: }
144: 
145: func (q *rateLimitingType[T]) Forget(item T) {
146: 	q.rateLimiter.Forget(item)
147: }

17: package cache
18: 
19: import (
20: 	"context"
21: 	"errors"
22: 	"fmt"
23: 	"sync"
24: 	"time"
25: 
26: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime"
27: 	utilruntime "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
28: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"
29: 	clientgofeaturegate "k8s.io/client-go/features"
30: 	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
31: )
32: 
33: // This file implements a low-level controller that is used in
34: // sharedIndexInformer, which is an implementation of
35: // SharedIndexInformer.  Such informers, in turn, are key components
36: // in the high level controllers that form the backbone of the
37: // Kubernetes control plane.  Look at those for examples, or the
38: // example in
39: // https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/tree/master/examples/workqueue
40: // .
41: 
42: // Config contains all the settings for one of these low-level controllers.
43: type Config struct {
44: 	// The queue for your objects - has to be a DeltaFIFO due to
45: 	// assumptions in the implementation. Your Process() function
46: 	// should accept the output of this Queue's Pop() method.
47: 	Queue
48: 
49: 	// Something that can list and watch your objects.
50: 	ListerWatcher
51: 
52: 	// Process can process a popped Deltas.
53: 	Process ProcessFunc
54: 
55: 	// ProcessBatch can process a batch of popped Deltas, which should return `TransactionError` if not all items
56: 	// in the batch were successfully processed.
57: 	//
58: 	// For batch processing to be used:
59: 	// * ProcessBatch must be non-nil
60: 	// * Queue must implement QueueWithBatch
61: 	// * The client InOrderInformersBatchProcess feature gate must be enabled
62: 	//
63: 	// If any of those are false, Process is used and no batch processing is done.
64: 	ProcessBatch ProcessBatchFunc
65: 
66: 	// ObjectType is an example object of the type this controller is
67: 	// expected to handle.
68: 	ObjectType runtime.Object
69: 
70: 	// ObjectDescription is the description to use when logging type-specific information about this controller.
71: 	ObjectDescription string
72: 
73: 	// FullResyncPeriod is the period at which ShouldResync is considered.
74: 	FullResyncPeriod time.Duration
75: 
76: 	// MinWatchTimeout, if set, will define the minimum timeout for watch requests send
77: 	// to kube-apiserver. However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid
78: 	// negative performance impact on controlplane.
79: 	// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.
80: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
81: 
82: 	// ShouldResync is periodically used by the reflector to determine
83: 	// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or
84: 	// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the
85: 	// resync.
86: 	ShouldResync ShouldResyncFunc
87: 
88: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
89: 	//
90: 	// Contextual logging: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext should be used instead of WatchErrorHandler in code which supports contextual logging.
91: 	WatchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandler
92: 
93: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error
94: 	// and WatchErrorHandler is not set.
95: 	WatchErrorHandlerWithContext WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
96: 
97: 	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.
98: 	WatchListPageSize int64
99: }
100: 
101: // ShouldResyncFunc is a type of function that indicates if a reflector should perform a

201: 	// The WatchErrorHandler is called whenever ListAndWatch drops the

291: // NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions creates a new instance for the ListerWatcher.
292: // The created informer will not do resyncs if options.ResyncPeriod is zero.  Otherwise: for each
293: // handler that with a non-zero requested resync period, whether added
294: // before or after the informer starts, the nominal resync period is
295: // the requested resync period rounded up to a multiple of the
296: // informer's resync checking period.  Such an informer's resync
297: // checking period is established when the informer starts running,
298: // and is the maximum of (a) the minimum of the resync periods
299: // requested before the informer starts and the
300: // options.ResyncPeriod given here and (b) the constant
301: // `minimumResyncPeriod` defined in this file.
302: func NewSharedIndexInformerWithOptions(lw ListerWatcher, exampleObject runtime.Object, options SharedIndexInformerOptions) SharedIndexInformer {
303: 	realClock := &clock.RealClock{}
304: 
305: 	return &sharedIndexInformer{
306: 		indexer:                         NewIndexer(DeletionHandlingMetaNamespaceKeyFunc, options.Indexers),
307: 		processor:                       &sharedProcessor{clock: realClock},
308: 		listerWatcher:                   lw,
309: 		objectType:                      exampleObject,
310: 		objectDescription:               options.ObjectDescription,
311: 		resyncCheckPeriod:               options.ResyncPeriod,
312: 		defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod: options.ResyncPeriod,
313: 		clock:                           realClock,
314: 		cacheMutationDetector:           NewCacheMutationDetector(fmt.Sprintf("%T", exampleObject)),
315: 	}
316: }

333: type InformerSynced func() bool

336: 	// syncedPollPeriod controls how often you look at the status of your sync funcs

343: // WaitForNamedCacheSync is a wrapper around WaitForCacheSync that generates log messages
344: // indicating that the caller identified by name is waiting for syncs, followed by
345: // either a successful or failed sync.
346: //
347: // Contextual logging: WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext should be used instead of WaitForNamedCacheSync in code which supports contextual logging.
348: func WaitForNamedCacheSync(controllerName string, stopCh <-chan struct{}, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
349: 	klog.Background().Info("Waiting for caches to sync", "controller", controllerName)
350: 
351: 	if !WaitForCacheSync(stopCh, cacheSyncs...) {
352: 		utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext(context.Background(), nil, "Unable to sync caches", "controller", controllerName)
353: 		return false
354: 	}
355: 
356: 	klog.Background().Info("Caches are synced", "controller", controllerName)
357: 	return true
358: }
359: 
360: // WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext is a wrapper around WaitForCacheSyncWithContext that generates log messages
361: // indicating that the caller is waiting for syncs, followed by either a successful or failed sync.
362: //
363: // Contextual logging can be used to identify the caller in those log messages. The log level is zero,
364: // the same as in [WaitForNamedCacheSync]. If this is too verbose, then store a logger with an increased
365: // threshold in the context:
366: //
367: //	WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext(klog.NewContext(ctx, logger.V(5)), ...)
368: func WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext(ctx context.Context, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
369: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
370: 	logger.Info("Waiting for caches to sync")
371: 
372: 	if !WaitForCacheSync(ctx.Done(), cacheSyncs...) {
373: 		utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext(ctx, nil, "Unable to sync caches")
374: 		return false
375: 	}
376: 
377: 	logger.Info("Caches are synced")
378: 	return true
379: }
380: 
381: // WaitForCacheSync waits for caches to populate.  It returns true if it was successful, false
382: // if the controller should shutdown
383: // callers should prefer WaitForNamedCacheSync()
384: func WaitForCacheSync(stopCh <-chan struct{}, cacheSyncs ...InformerSynced) bool {
385: 	err := wait.PollImmediateUntil(syncedPollPeriod,
386: 		func() (bool, error) {
387: 			for _, syncFunc := range cacheSyncs {
388: 				if !syncFunc() {
389: 					return false, nil
390: 				}
391: 			}
392: 			return true, nil
393: 		},
394: 		stopCh)
395: 	if err != nil {
396: 		return false
397: 	}
398: 
399: 	return true
400: }

402: // `*sharedIndexInformer` implements SharedIndexInformer and has three
403: // main components.  One is an indexed local cache, `indexer Indexer`.
404: // The second main component is a Controller that pulls
405: // objects/notifications using the ListerWatcher and pushes them into
406: // a DeltaFIFO --- whose knownObjects is the informer's local cache
407: // --- while concurrently Popping Deltas values from that fifo and
408: // processing them with `sharedIndexInformer::HandleDeltas`.  Each
409: // invocation of HandleDeltas, which is done with the fifo's lock
410: // held, processes each Delta in turn.  For each Delta this both
411: // updates the local cache and stuffs the relevant notification into
412: // the sharedProcessor.  The third main component is that
413: // sharedProcessor, which is responsible for relaying those
414: // notifications to each of the informer's clients.
415: type sharedIndexInformer struct {
416: 	indexer    Indexer
417: 	controller Controller
418: 
419: 	processor             *sharedProcessor
420: 	cacheMutationDetector MutationDetector
421: 
422: 	listerWatcher ListerWatcher
423: 
424: 	// objectType is an example object of the type this informer is expected to handle. If set, an event
425: 	// with an object with a mismatching type is dropped instead of being delivered to listeners.
426: 	objectType runtime.Object
427: 
428: 	// objectDescription is the description of this informer's objects. This typically defaults to
429: 	objectDescription string
430: 
431: 	// resyncCheckPeriod is how often we want the reflector's resync timer to fire so it can call
432: 	// shouldResync to check if any of our listeners need a resync.
433: 	resyncCheckPeriod time.Duration
434: 	// defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod is the default resync period for any handlers added via
435: 	// AddEventHandler (i.e. they don't specify one and just want to use the shared informer's default
436: 	// value).
437: 	defaultEventHandlerResyncPeriod time.Duration
438: 	// clock allows for testability
439: 	clock clock.Clock
440: 
441: 	started, stopped bool
442: 	startedLock      sync.Mutex
443: 
444: 	// blockDeltas gives a way to stop all event distribution so that a late event handler
445: 	// can safely join the shared informer.
446: 	blockDeltas sync.Mutex
447: 
448: 	// Called whenever the ListAndWatch drops the connection with an error.
449: 	watchErrorHandler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext
450: 
451: 	transform TransformFunc
452: }

457: // Because returning information back is always asynchronous, the legacy callers shouldn't

26: // Indexer extends Store with multiple indices and restricts each
27: // accumulator to simply hold the current object (and be empty after
28: // Delete).
29: //
30: // There are three kinds of strings here:
31: //  1. a storage key, as defined in the Store interface,
32: //  2. a name of an index, and
33: //  3. an "indexed value", which is produced by an IndexFunc and
34: //     can be a field value or any other string computed from the object.
35: type Indexer interface {
36: 	Store
37: 	// Index returns the stored objects whose set of indexed values
38: 	// intersects the set of indexed values of the given object, for
39: 	// the named index
40: 	Index(indexName string, obj interface{}) ([]interface{}, error)
41: 	// IndexKeys returns the storage keys of the stored objects whose
42: 	// set of indexed values for the named index includes the given
43: 	// indexed value
44: 	IndexKeys(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]string, error)
45: 	// ListIndexFuncValues returns all the indexed values of the given index
46: 	ListIndexFuncValues(indexName string) []string
47: 	// ByIndex returns the stored objects whose set of indexed values
48: 	// for the named index includes the given indexed value
49: 	ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue string) ([]interface{}, error)
50: 	// GetIndexers return the indexers
51: 	GetIndexers() Indexers
52: 
53: 	// AddIndexers adds more indexers to this store. This supports adding indexes after the store already has items.
54: 	AddIndexers(newIndexers Indexers) error
55: }
56: 
57: // IndexFunc knows how to compute the set of indexed values for an object.
58: type IndexFunc func(obj interface{}) ([]string, error)
59: 
60: // IndexFuncToKeyFuncAdapter adapts an indexFunc to a keyFunc.  This is only useful if your index function returns
61: // unique values for every object.  This conversion can create errors when more than one key is found.  You
62: // should prefer to make proper key and index functions.
63: func IndexFuncToKeyFuncAdapter(indexFunc IndexFunc) KeyFunc {
64: 	return func(obj interface{}) (string, error) {
65: 		indexKeys, err := indexFunc(obj)
66: 		if err != nil {
67: 			return "", err
68: 		}
69: 		if len(indexKeys) > 1 {
70: 			return "", fmt.Errorf("too many keys: %v", indexKeys)
71: 		}
72: 		if len(indexKeys) == 0 {
73: 			return "", fmt.Errorf("unexpected empty indexKeys")
74: 		}
75: 		return indexKeys[0], nil
76: 	}
77: }

85: func MetaNamespaceIndexFunc(obj interface{}) ([]string, error) {
86: 	meta, err := meta.Accessor(obj)
87: 	if err != nil {
88: 		return []string{""}, fmt.Errorf("object has no meta: %v", err)
89: 	}
90: 	return []string{meta.GetNamespace()}, nil

17: // Package leaderelection implements leader election of a set of endpoints.
18: // It uses an annotation in the endpoints object to store the record of the
19: // election state. This implementation does not guarantee that only one
20: // client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing).
21: //
22: // A client only acts on timestamps captured locally to infer the state of the
23: // leader election. The client does not consider timestamps in the leader
24: // election record to be accurate because these timestamps may not have been
25: // produced by a local clock. The implemention does not depend on their
26: // accuracy and only uses their change to indicate that another client has
27: // renewed the leader lease. Thus the implementation is tolerant to arbitrary
28: // clock skew, but is not tolerant to arbitrary clock skew rate.
29: //
30: // However the level of tolerance to skew rate can be configured by setting
31: // RenewDeadline and LeaseDuration appropriately. The tolerance expressed as a
32: // maximum tolerated ratio of time passed on the fastest node to time passed on
33: // the slowest node can be approximately achieved with a configuration that sets
34: // the same ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline. For example if a user wanted
35: // to tolerate some nodes progressing forward in time twice as fast as other nodes,
36: // the user could set LeaseDuration to 60 seconds and RenewDeadline to 30 seconds.
37: //
38: // While not required, some method of clock synchronization between nodes in the
39: // cluster is highly recommended. It's important to keep in mind when configuring
40: // this client that the tolerance to skew rate varies inversely to master
41: // availability.
42: //
43: // Larger clusters often have a more lenient SLA for API latency. This should be
44: // taken into account when configuring the client. The rate of leader transitions
45: // should be monitored and RetryPeriod and LeaseDuration should be increased
46: // until the rate is stable and acceptably low. It's important to keep in mind
47: // when configuring this client that the tolerance to API latency varies inversely
48: // to master availability.
49: //
50: // DISCLAIMER: this is an alpha API. This library will likely change significantly
51: // or even be removed entirely in subsequent releases. Depend on this API at
52: // your own risk.
53: package leaderelection
54: 
55: import (
56: 	"bytes"
57: 	"context"
58: 	"fmt"
59: 	"sync"
60: 	"time"
61: 
62: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
63: 	metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
64: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
65: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"
66: 	rl "k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock"
67: 	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
68: 	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
69: )
70: 
71: const (
72: 	JitterFactor = 1.2
73: )
74: 
75: // NewLeaderElector creates a LeaderElector from a LeaderElectionConfig
76: func NewLeaderElector(lec LeaderElectionConfig) (*LeaderElector, error) {
77: 	if lec.LeaseDuration <= lec.RenewDeadline {
78: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than renewDeadline")
79: 	}
80: 	if lec.RenewDeadline <= time.Duration(JitterFactor*float64(lec.RetryPeriod)) {
81: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than retryPeriod*JitterFactor")
82: 	}
83: 	if lec.LeaseDuration < 1 {
84: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than zero")
85: 	}
86: 	if lec.RenewDeadline < 1 {
87: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than zero")
88: 	}
89: 	if lec.RetryPeriod < 1 {
90: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("retryPeriod must be greater than zero")
91: 	}
92: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading == nil {
93: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStartedLeading callback must not be nil")
94: 	}
95: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading == nil {
96: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStoppedLeading callback must not be nil")
97: 	}
98: 
99: 	if lec.Lock == nil {
100: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Lock must not be nil.")
101: 	}
102: 	id := lec.Lock.Identity()
103: 	if id == "" {
104: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Lock identity is empty")
105: 	}
106: 
107: 	le := LeaderElector{
108: 		config:  lec,
109: 		clock:   clock.RealClock{},
110: 		metrics: globalMetricsFactory.newLeaderMetrics(),
111: 	}
112: 	le.metrics.leaderOff(le.config.Name)
113: 	return &le, nil
114: }
115: 
116: type LeaderElectionConfig struct {
117: 	// Lock is the resource that will be used for locking
118: 	Lock rl.Interface
119: 
120: 	// LeaseDuration is the duration that non-leader candidates will
121: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kubernetes--stripped@6d5678ae074d17945f174da2b064603131370a5f/-/blob/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go%3FL1-120"}

{"content":"120: 	// LeaseDuration is the duration that non-leader candidates will
121: 	// wait to force acquire leadership. This is measured against time of
122: 	// last observed ack.
123: 	//
124: 	// A client needs to wait a full LeaseDuration without observing a change to
125: 	// the record before it can attempt to take over. When all clients are
126: 	// shutdown and a new set of clients are started with different names against
127: 	// the same leader record, they must wait the full LeaseDuration before
128: 	// attempting to acquire the lease. Thus LeaseDuration should be as short as
129: 	// possible (within your tolerance for clock skew rate) to avoid a possible
130: 	// long waits in the scenario.
131: 	//
132: 	// Core clients default this value to 15 seconds.
133: 	LeaseDuration time.Duration
134: 	// RenewDeadline is the duration that the acting master will retry
135: 	// refreshing leadership before giving up.
136: 	//
137: 	// Core clients default this value to 10 seconds.
138: 	RenewDeadline time.Duration
139: 	// RetryPeriod is the duration the LeaderElector clients should wait
140: 	// between tries of actions.
141: 	//
142: 	// Core clients default this value to 2 seconds.
143: 	RetryPeriod time.Duration
144: 
145: 	// Callbacks are callbacks that are triggered during certain lifecycle
146: 	// events of the LeaderElector
147: 	Callbacks LeaderCallbacks
148: 
149: 	// WatchDog is the associated health checker
150: 	// WatchDog may be null if it's not needed/configured.
151: 	WatchDog *HealthzAdaptor
152: 
153: 	// ReleaseOnCancel should be set true if the lock should be released
154: 	// when the run context is cancelled. If you set this to true, you must
155: 	// ensure all code guarded by this lease has successfully completed
156: 	// prior to cancelling the context, or you may have two processes
157: 	// simultaneously acting on the critical path.
158: 	ReleaseOnCancel bool
159: 
160: 	// Name is the name of the resource lock for debugging
161: 	Name string
162: 
163: 	// Coordinated will use the Coordinated Leader Election feature
164: 	// WARNING: Coordinated leader election is ALPHA.
165: 	Coordinated bool
166: }
167: 
168: // LeaderCallbacks are callbacks that are triggered during certain
169: // lifecycle events of the LeaderElector. These are invoked asynchronously.
170: //
171: // possible future callbacks:
172: //   - OnChallenge()
173: type LeaderCallbacks struct {
174: 	// OnStartedLeading is called when a LeaderElector client starts leading
175: 	OnStartedLeading func(context.Context)
176: 	// OnStoppedLeading is called when a LeaderElector client stops leading.
177: 	// This callback is always called when the LeaderElector exits, even if it did not start leading.
178: 	// Users should not assume that OnStoppedLeading is only called after OnStartedLeading.
179: 	// see: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/127675#discussion_r1780059887
180: 	OnStoppedLeading func()
181: 	// OnNewLeader is called when the client observes a leader that is
182: 	// not the previously observed leader. This includes the first observed
183: 	// leader when the client starts.
184: 	OnNewLeader func(identity string)
185: }
186: 
187: // LeaderElector is a leader election client.
188: type LeaderElector struct {
189: 	config LeaderElectionConfig
190: 	// internal bookkeeping
191: 	observedRecord    rl.LeaderElectionRecord
192: 	observedRawRecord []byte
193: 	observedTime      time.Time
194: 	// used to implement OnNewLeader(), may lag slightly from the
195: 	// value observedRecord.HolderIdentity if the transition has
196: 	// not yet been reported.
197: 	reportedLeader string
198: 
199: 	// clock is wrapper around time to allow for less flaky testing
200: 	clock clock.Clock
201: 
202: 	// used to lock the observedRecord
203: 	observedRecordLock sync.Mutex
204: 
205: 	metrics leaderMetricsAdapter
206: }
207: 
208: // Run starts the leader election loop. Run will not return
209: // before leader election loop is stopped by ctx or it has
210: // stopped holding the leader lease
211: func (le *LeaderElector) Run(ctx context.Context) {
212: 	defer runtime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
213: 	defer le.config.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading()
214: 
215: 	if !le.acquire(ctx) {
216: 		return // ctx signalled done
217: 	}
218: 	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
219: 	defer cancel()
220: 	go le.config.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading(ctx)
221: 	le.renew(ctx)
222: }
223: 
224: // RunOrDie starts a client with the provided config or panics if the config
225: // fails to validate. RunOrDie blocks until leader election loop is
226: // stopped by ctx or it has stopped holding the leader lease
227: func RunOrDie(ctx context.Context, lec LeaderElectionConfig) {
228: 	le, err := NewLeaderElector(lec)
229: 	if err != nil {
230: 		panic(err)
231: 	}
232: 	if lec.WatchDog != nil {
233: 		lec.WatchDog.SetLeaderElection(le)
234: 	}
235: 	le.Run(ctx)
236: }
237: 
238: // GetLeader returns the identity of the last observed leader or returns the empty string if
239: // no leader has yet been observed.
240: // This function is for informational purposes. (e.g. monitoring, logs, etc.)
241: func (le *LeaderElector) GetLeader() string {
242: 	return le.getObservedRecord().HolderIdentity
243: }
244: 
245: // IsLeader returns true if the last observed leader was this client else returns false.
246: func (le *LeaderElector) IsLeader() bool {
247: 	return le.getObservedRecord().HolderIdentity == le.config.Lock.Identity()
248: }
249: 
250: // acquire loops calling tryAcquireOrRenew and returns true immediately when tryAcquireOrRenew succeeds.
251: // Returns false if ctx signals done.
252: func (le *LeaderElector) acquire(ctx context.Context) bool {
253: 	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
254: 	defer cancel()
255: 	succeeded := false
256: 	desc := le.config.Lock.Describe()
257: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
258: 	logger.Info("Attempting to acquire leader lease...", "lock", desc)
259: 	wait.JitterUntilWithContext(ctx, func(ctx context.Context) {
260: 		if !le.config.Coordinated {
261: 			succeeded = le.tryAcquireOrRenew(ctx)
262: 		} else {
263: 			succeeded = le.tryCoordinatedRenew(ctx)
264: 		}
265: 		le.maybeReportTransition()
266: 		if !succeeded {
267: 			logger.V(4).Info("Failed to acquire lease", "lock", desc)
268: 			return
269: 		}
270: 		le.config.Lock.RecordEvent("became leader")
271: 		le.metrics.leaderOn(le.config.Name)
272: 		logger.Info("Successfully acquired lease", "lock", desc)
273: 		cancel()
274: 	}, le.config.RetryPeriod, JitterFactor, true)
275: 	return succeeded
276: }
277: 
278: // renew loops calling tryAcquireOrRenew and returns immediately when tryAcquireOrRenew fails or ctx signals done.
279: func (le *LeaderElector) renew(ctx context.Context) {
280: 	defer le.config.Lock.RecordEvent("stopped leading")

34: // New returns an http.RoundTripper that will provide the authentication
35: // or transport level security defined by the provided Config.
36: func New(config *Config) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
37: 	// Set transport level security
38: 	if config.Transport != nil && (config.HasCA() || config.HasCertAuth() || config.HasCertCallback() || config.TLS.Insecure) {
39: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("using a custom transport with TLS certificate options or the insecure flag is not allowed")
40: 	}
41: 
42: 	if !isValidHolders(config) {
43: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("misconfigured holder for dialer or cert callback")
44: 	}
45: 
46: 	var (
47: 		rt  http.RoundTripper
48: 		err error
49: 	)
50: 
51: 	if config.Transport != nil {
52: 		rt = config.Transport
53: 	} else {
54: 		rt, err = tlsCache.get(config)
55: 		if err != nil {
56: 			return nil, err
57: 		}
58: 	}
59: 
60: 	return HTTPWrappersForConfig(config, rt)
61: }
62: 
63: func isValidHolders(config *Config) bool {
64: 	if config.TLS.GetCertHolder != nil && config.TLS.GetCertHolder.GetCert == nil {
65: 		return false
66: 	}
67: 
68: 	if config.DialHolder != nil && config.DialHolder.Dial == nil {
69: 		return false
70: 	}
71: 
72: 	return true
73: }

27: // Deprecated: RateLimiter is deprecated, use TypedRateLimiter instead.
28: type RateLimiter TypedRateLimiter[any]
29: 
30: type TypedRateLimiter[T comparable] interface {
31: 	// When gets an item and gets to decide how long that item should wait
32: 	When(item T) time.Duration
33: 	// Forget indicates that an item is finished being retried.  Doesn't matter whether it's for failing
34: 	// or for success, we'll stop tracking it
35: 	Forget(item T)
36: 	// NumRequeues returns back how many failures the item has had
37: 	NumRequeues(item T) int
38: }
39: 
40: // DefaultControllerRateLimiter is a no-arg constructor for a default rate limiter for a workqueue.  It has
41: // both overall and per-item rate limiting.  The overall is a token bucket and the per-item is exponential
42: //
43: // Deprecated: Use DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter instead.
44: func DefaultControllerRateLimiter() RateLimiter {
45: 	return DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter[any]()
46: }
47: 
48: // DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter is a no-arg constructor for a default rate limiter for a workqueue.  It has
49: // both overall and per-item rate limiting.  The overall is a token bucket and the per-item is exponential
50: func DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter[T comparable]() TypedRateLimiter[T] {
51: 	return NewTypedMaxOfRateLimiter(
52: 		NewTypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[T](5*time.Millisecond, 1000*time.Second),
53: 		// 10 qps, 100 bucket size.  This is only for retry speed and its only the overall factor (not per item)
54: 		&TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]{Limiter: rate.NewLimiter(rate.Limit(10), 100)},
55: 	)
56: }
57: 
58: // Deprecated: BucketRateLimiter is deprecated, use TypedBucketRateLimiter instead.
59: type BucketRateLimiter = TypedBucketRateLimiter[any]

61: // TypedBucketRateLimiter adapts a standard bucket to the workqueue ratelimiter API
62: type TypedBucketRateLimiter[T comparable] struct {
63: 	*rate.Limiter
64: }
65: 
66: var _ RateLimiter = &BucketRateLimiter{}
67: 
68: func (r *TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]) When(item T) time.Duration {
69: 	return r.Limiter.Reserve().Delay()
70: }
71: 
72: func (r *TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]) NumRequeues(item T) int {
73: 	return 0
74: }
75: 
76: func (r *TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]) Forget(item T) {
77: }

80: type ItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter = TypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[any]

83: // dealing with max failures and expiration are up to the caller

85: 	failuresLock sync.Mutex

90: }

92: var _ RateLimiter = &ItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter{}

96: 	return NewTypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[any](baseDelay, maxDelay)
97: }

101: 		failures:  map[T]int{},

103: 		maxDelay:  maxDelay,
104: 	}
105: }

114: }

117: 	r.failuresLock.Lock()
118: 	defer r.failuresLock.Unlock()
